ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for wholemeal bread with kaya spread; mapped to the closest parent class 'bread food product' (FOODON_03315952). A more specific term for wholemeal bread with coconut jam spread is not available in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for kaya-spread wholemeal bread. A000E (bread and rolls) is the closest defensible parent. Kaya (coconut egg jam) is a Southeast Asian condiment not separately coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for wholemeal bread with kaya spread as a composite food item. SNOMED CT food concepts are limited for composite Southeast Asian dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of wholemeal bread (approximately 80g total) spread with kaya, commonly consumed as a breakfast item in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with kaya — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (F05020140)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F05020140 for wholemeal bread with kaya. All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (wholemeal bread + kaya spread components) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wholemeal/whole wheat bread analogous entry; University of Sydney GI Database reference for wholemeal bread (GI ~69)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for wholemeal/whole wheat bread, which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. Kaya spread contributes additional sugar but does not have a separate GI entry; the composite GI is estimated as medium (69) based on the wholemeal bread base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 34.25) / 100 ≈ 23.6, using 80g serving with 42.81g carbohydrate per 100g.

hypertension.key_factor: Elevated sugar (10.82g/100g) exceeds the 10g/100g solid-food sugar threshold; sodium at 290mg/100g is borderline elevated; potassium is very low at 21mg/100g providing no meaningful sodium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit kaya spread due to its high sugar content and the absence of potassium to counterbalance sodium; replacing kaya with avocado or unsweetened nut butter is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 10.82g/100g from kaya (coconut egg jam) combined with a medium GI of 69 and glycemic load of 23.6 per serving will cause meaningful post-meal blood glucose elevation despite the moderating effect of 4.66g/100g dietary fibre from wholemeal bread.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit kaya to no more than one teaspoon per slice or substitute with unsweetened nut butter, and monitor post-meal blood glucose when consuming this food.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is moderate at 5.34g/100g; however, kaya contains coconut-derived saturated fat (estimated ~1.8g/100g) and cholesterol at 19.57mg/100g from eggs; sodium at 290mg/100g adds cardiovascular load; dietary fibre at 4.66g/100g from wholemeal bread is a positive factor.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit portion size and frequency of kaya consumption due to its saturated fat and sugar content; the wholemeal bread base provides beneficial dietary fibre that partially offsets cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with kaya is a popular Singaporean breakfast dish consisting of sliced wholemeal (whole wheat) bread spread with kaya, a traditional Southeast Asian coconut egg jam made from coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan leaf. The bread provides a dense, slightly nutty texture with a chewy crumb, while the kaya spread adds a sweet, fragrant, custard-like flavour with a smooth, thick consistency. Kaya is a culturally iconic condiment in Singapore and Malaysia, commonly served at traditional kopitiam (coffee shops) alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (tea). The wholemeal variant offers higher dietary fibre (4.66g/100g) and more micronutrients compared to white bread versions, making it a marginally more nutritious choice. Per 100g, this dish provides 258 kcal, 10.42g protein, 5.34g fat, 42.81g carbohydrate, 10.82g sugar, 4.66g dietary fibre, 290mg sodium, 146mg calcium, and 4.73mg iron. The medium glycemic index of 69 and glycemic load of approximately 23.6 per 80g serving indicate a moderate glycaemic impact. Clinically, the high sugar content from kaya is a concern for individuals with type 2 diabetes and hypertension, while the wholemeal base provides beneficial fibre. This dish is suitable as an occasional breakfast item for healthy individuals but should be consumed with caution by those managing blood glucose or blood pressure.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record F05020140 with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for wholemeal bread as the analogous base food. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and is the only value not directly from HPB.
notes: Saturated fat (1.8g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy for wholemeal bread and kaya spread components, as HPB record F05020140 does not separately report saturated fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record F05020140 for this composite dish, and no sufficiently close approved-source analogue was available to provide defensible estimates for all these micronutrients. Potassium (21mg/100g), calcium (146mg/100g), iron (4.73mg/100g), and cholesterol (19.57mg/100g) are reported directly from HPB. The GI value of 69 is an analogous estimate based on wholemeal bread from the University of Sydney GI Database; the kaya spread component does not have a separate GI entry. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 80g serving size: GL = (69 × 42.81 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 23.6. This dish is a Singaporean dish and therefore requires English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi multilingual coverage, all of which are provided.
